title: [Quadlet] Failed to enable unit: Unit xyz is transient or generated.
body:
### Issue Description

I can't enable the service of my .kube file.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `cp dyndns-https-ip-pod.kube ~/.config/containers/systemd/`
2. `systemctl --user daemon-reload`
3. `systemctl --user enable --now dyndns-https-ip-pod.service`


### Describe the results you received

```
$ systemctl --user enable --now dyndns-https-ip-pod.service
Failed to enable unit: Unit /run/user/1000/systemd/generator/dyndns-https-ip-pod.service is transient or generated.
```

### Describe the results you expected

A working service

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

### Additional environment details

### Additional information

```
# ~/.config/containers/systemd/dyndns-https-ip-pod.kube

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target

[Kube]
Yaml=/opt/podman/ServerContainerTemplate/container/services/dynds-https-ip/dyndns-https-ip-pod.yaml

[Unit]
After=network-online.target
Description=HTTPS & DynDNS support
Wants=network-online.target
```

comments.body: @ygalblum PTAL

Ah I think I misunderstood the meaning of this error.
So if I understand it correctly it's enough to just `start` the service and the `[Install]` section will start it on the system boot?

Then probably the podman quadlet manual needs a hint / update?

From the added section to the first answer in this issue: https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/354681/init-service-failing-to-enable-once-a-systemd-service-file-is-generated I understand that generated unit files are not meant to be enabled on their own but instead be enabled using a target in the `Install` section. If my understanding is correct, this aligns with @MartinX3's comment.
@alexlarsson is our understanding correct?
Oh, according to your link even generated systemD services should be able to get enabled if they have an install section.

It is not possible to enable a generated service yes, because they are in general not persistent identifiers. In the case of quadlet, the generator itself handles enabling though, so if the `*.container` file has an [Install] section, the correct links will be generated to automatically start the service.

In other words, it is like the enable is run automatically.
So, in the example above, which has an Install section in the .kube file, you need only install the file and start it. It will be automatically enabled and started next boot.

Thank you very much.
I think the man page should get extended to contain a hint.
This means that the user must link the service to another service or target, right? What would be the best practice here?
@ygalblum What do you mean?

So Alex are you saying, all he needed to do was

1.     cp dyndns-https-ip-pod.kube ~/.config/containers/systemd/
2.     systemctl --user daemon-reload
3.     systemctl --user start dyndns-https-ip-pod.service

Reboot 

And the service would be running?
@rhatdan Sort of. If you reboot you need only 1, but if you also want to run the service before rebooting you also need 2-3.

Ok so the docs should be stated that moving the unit file into the appropriate directory is equivalent of an enable service.
IE If the machine reboots after the file was moved in place, the service will be started.

If you want to start the service without reboot, then  you need to do

systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl start $NAME.service

> My question was about how we would document it. Since the user cannot enable the service by calling systemctl enable she will have to set the Install section. This means that the service will be attached to the start sequence of a different service or target, but never on its own. Am I correct? Is there a way to make it standalone (i.e. add a dummy service that will be set as WantedBy in the Quadlet one)?

Lets take a step back. For a normal unit, if there is a section in the foo.service file like:
```
[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```
Then nothing happens initially, and the unit only starts if you manually start it, or some other service depends on it.

However, when you run `systemctl enable foo.service` on the unit, systemd reads the `Install` section and applies it. In this case it will create a symlink `.../default.target.wants/foo.service` -> `.../foo.service`.

This means that on startup, the service will be started (because default.target is part of a normal boot). This is just how systemd works, nothing specific to quadlet. 

However, `systemctl enable` doesn't work for a generated file, so you can't run `systemctl enable` on the service generated by quadlet. For this reason, the quadlet generator does the equivalent of `systemctl enable`at boot. I.e. creates the required symlinks.

So, yes, this means that the quadlet unit needs to "be attached to the start sequence of a different service or target", but that is true for any unit file, quadlet or not. And they way this is fixed is to use the standard targets like "default.target" or "graphical.target", etc.

Thanks for the explanation. I did not how the `Install` part works.

Now, I'm trying to think about different use cases. 

Let's assume I have a service that I want to distribute without enabling it by default. But, I want to allow the user to be able to enable it whenever they want to. How will I, as the writer and distributor or the `.container` file, do that?
Not include an `Install` section? If so, then I need to explain the user that they need to add the section themselves in order to enable it. Is that right?
Alternatively, should the unit file have a key for auto-enable? It will still require the user to edit the file in order to have Quadlet enable the service, but one that is easier to explain.

WDYT?

Yeah, shipping a non-enabled file currently means no install section.
We could have an option to disable the automatic apply of the install section, but then nothing can read it (because systemctl enable on the generated file doesn't work), so as you say, users would have to edit the file to enable it. Alternatively we could maybe have a `.d` style support in the generator where we can add the install snippet in a separate file (which you can chose to install or not).
Another option would be to have a `podman quadlet enable foo.container` thing that parses the otherwise disabled Install section and creates the right symlinks. That might work.
I think we should combine the discussions between #17662 and #17567. So, I opened a new discussion #17744
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
/remove stale
We're waiting for a response from the systemd team. In any case, this issue is now being tracked in #17744 and therefore, I'm closing this one
